sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=29, col=38).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 20, level 1.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 19 actions; a strong score needs about 7 or fewer. You are 2.7x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Let me analyze what I've learned:
1. Row 0 has 41 P (pink) pixels and 23 c (charcoal) pixels = 64 total
2. The progress bar seems to be filling up with charcoal pixels as I click
3. Clicking the top yellow strip didn't trigger level completion
The progress bar is 64 pixels wide. It started with 0 charcoal pixels and now has 23. If the goal is to fill the progress bar completely (64 charcoal pixels), that would require many more clicks, which doesn't fit the efficiency budget.
Wait, maybe the progress bar isn't the goal. Let me reconsider the puzzle.
Looking at the current state:
- Charcoal square at [44,38]-[49,41]
- Top yellow at [28,38]-[31,39]
- The charcoal is now directly below the top yellow (both start at col 38)
But the level didn't complete. Maybe I need to think about this differently.
Let me look at the board layout again:
- There's a dark gray region on the left and white on the right
- The boundary between dark gray and white is around col 32 (based on the initial state where the charcoal was at col 46, in the white region)
Wait, let me reconsider the initial state:
- Charcoal at [44,46]-[49,49] - this is in the white region (right side)
- After moving left 2 times (8 columns), charcoal at [44,38]-[49,41] - this spans the boundary
Hmm, maybe the goal is to move the charcoal square to the left side (dark gray region)? Let me try moving it further left.
Actually, wait. Let me look at the board more carefully. The dark gray region is on the left, and the white region is on the right. The charcoal square started in the white region. Maybe the goal is to move it to the dark gray region?
Let me check where the boundary is. Looking at the initial state:
- Left black bar: [28,20]-[31,37]
- Top yellow: [28,38]-[31,39]
- Right black bar: [28,40]-[31,63]
The black bars and yellow strip are all in rows 28-31. The charcoal is in rows 44-49.
Let me think about this differently. What if the goal is to move the charcoal square so it's directly below the top yellow strip, and then click something specific? Or what if the goal is to move the charcoal square to a position where it's completely within the dark gray region?
Let me try moving the charcoal further left, into the dark gray region. The charcoal is currently at cols 38-41. If I move it left 2 more times (8 columns), it would be at cols 30-33, which might be in the dark gray region.
Actually, let me reconsider the whole puzzle. Maybe the goal is not about moving the charcoal at all. Maybe the goal is about the progress bar. The progress bar is filling up with charcoal pixels. If I need to fill it completely, that would take 64 clicks (since each click adds about 1-2 pixels). But that's way too many for the efficiency budget.
Wait, let me look at the progress bar more carefully. It has 41 P pixels and 23 c pixels. The P pixels are on the left and the c pixels are on the right. So the progress bar is filling from right to left? Or is it filling from left to right with c pixels replacing P pixels?
Actually, looking at the row 0 string: "PPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPP...Pccccccccccccccccccccccc"
The P pixels are on the left (41 of them) and the c pixels are on the right (23 of them). So the progress bar is filling from right to left with c pixels.
If the goal is to fill the progress bar completely with c pixels, that would take 41 more clicks (to replace all 41 P pixels). But that's still too many.
Let me try a completely different hypothesis. What if the goal is to move the charcoal square to a specific position and then the level completes automatically? Let me try moving the charcoal to the far left edge.
Actually, let me reconsider the problem. The efficiency budget says I need about 7 actions. I've already used 19. So I need to find the solution quickly.
Let me think about what 7 actions could accomplish:
1. Move the charcoal to a specific position (maybe 2-3 moves)
2. Click something specific (1-2 clicks)
Or maybe the solution is simpler: just move the charcoal to a specific position and the level completes.
Let me try moving the charcoal to the far left. Currently at cols 38-41. If I move it left 10 times, it would be at cols -2 to 1, which would be off the board. So there must be a limit.
Actually, let me try moving the charcoal left until it hits the left edge and see what happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed after clicking top yellow
before = last_transition.before_frame
after = last_transition.after_frame

def count_color(frame, color):
    return sum(1 for row in frame.ascii.split('\n') for c in row if c == color)

print(f"Yellow: {count_color(before,'Y')} -> {count_color(after,'Y')}")
print(f"Charcoal: {count_color(before,'c')} -> {count_color(after,'c')}")

# Check current progress bar
row0 = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')[0]
c_count = row0.count('c')
p_count = row0.count('P')
print(f"Progress: P={p_count}, c={c_count}")

# Let me try a different approach - move charcoal all the way left
# Currently at cols 38-41, move left until it hits the boundary
# Let's try moving left 10 times to see what happens
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 33, 'col': 61}] * 10)
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"MOUSE": "Top blue moves charcoal+yellow RIGHT 4. Bottom blue moves LEFT 4. Other clicks add pixels (yellow=2, others=1)."}, "object_taxonomy": {"P": "pink top bar", "G": "dark gray bg", "W": "white bg", "b": "blue squares (2) - movement buttons", "B": "black bars (2)", "Y": "yellow strips (2)", "c": "charcoal square (movable)"}, "hud_map": {"row_0": "progress bar (64 wide, fills with c from right)"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": ""}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Charcoal at cols 38-41. Progress bar 41P+23c. Need to find completion trigger.", "goal_model": "Move charcoal to specific position or fill progress bar", "recent_findings": ["Progress bar fills with clicks", "Clicking top yellow just added pixel"], "open_questions": ["What triggers level completion?"], "current_plan": "Move charcoal all the way left to see if it triggers completion", "strategy_log": ["clicked all objects - no finish", "discovered movement mechanic", "moved to right edge", "clicked charcoal - just added pixel", "moved left to align with top yellow", "clicked charcoal again - just added pixel", "clicked top yellow - just added pixel"], "failed_probes": ["clicked white bg", "clicked charcoal at right edge", "clicked charcoal at aligned position", "clicked top yellow"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
